All public street trees in the City of Boston and Massachusetts are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). The BPRD Urban Forestry Division is unable to remove a live and healthy public shade tree for the reason requested.

All public street trees in the City of Boston and Massachusetts are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). The BPRD Urban Forestry Division is unable to remove a live and healthy public shade tree for the reason requested. The only way this tree could be removed for the reason requested is if the Public Works Department was planning to reconstruct all of Beryl Street to make the sidewalks ADA compliant and to have public tree hearings for the possible removal of each shade tree on the street that is interfering with sidewalk passage. A case has been made to the Public Works Department to inspect the sidewalk for repair.

All public street trees in the City of Boston and Massachusetts are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). The BPRD Urban Forestry Division is unable to remove a live and healthy public shade tree for the reason requested. The only way this tree could be removed for the reason requested is if the Public Works Department was planning to reconstruct all of Fletcher Street to make the sidewalks ADA compliant and to have public tree hearings for the possible removal of each shade tree on the street that is interfering with sidewalk passage. You can contact the City Clerks office regarding any damages to your property. Any repairs can be completed by you and any tree roots impacting your property my be cut at the property line.

All public street trees in the City of Boston and Massachusetts are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). The BPRD Urban Forestry Division is unable to remove a live and healthy public shade tree for the reason requested. The only way this tree could be removed for the reason requested is if the Public Works Department was planning to reconstruct all of Glencliff Road to make the sidewalks ADA compliant and to have public tree hearings for the possible removal of each shade tree on the street that is interfering with sidewalk passage. A case has been made to the Public Works Department (Case #101006603109) to inspect the sidewalk for repair.

All public street trees in the City of Boston and Massachusetts are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). The BPRD Urban Forestry Division is unable to remove a live and healthy public shade tree for the reason requested. The tree in question currently meets the City of Boston pruning specifications for street trees and does not require further pruning at this time.

The BPRD Urban Forestry Division is unable to remove a live and healthy public shade tree for the reason requested. The tree is unable to be relocated at this location due to existing infrastructure.

All public street trees in the City of Boston and Massachusetts are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). The BPRD Urban Forestry Division is unable to remove a live and healthy public shade tree. This tree still has live buds and green twigs. If tree condition changes please contact our office at Trees@boston.gov.

The tree in question does not preseny indications of structural instability or soild movement at the time of inspection. The BPRD Urban Forestry Division is unable to remove a live and healthy public shade tree for the reason requested.

All public street trees in the City of Boston and Massachusetts are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). The tree in question is currently alive, with visible bud swell occuring. The BPRD Urban Forestry Division is unable to remove a live and healthy public shade tree for the reason requested.

All public street trees in the City of Boston are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). For the BPRD Urban Forestry Division to consider removing a public shade tree for the reason given, we need proof of the roots damaging the foundation. Proof shall include pictures of the roots growing through and damaging the foundation and written documentation from a structural engineer stating that the tree must be removed in order for the repairs to be completed. Until then the tree will not be removed. Proof of the damage can be sent to Boston Parks & Recreation Department 3rd floor, 1010 Massachusetts Ave Boston, MA 02118 Attn: Urban Forestry Division or emailed to Trees@boston.gov

All public street trees in the City of Boston are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). For the BPRD Urban Forestry Division to remove a live and healthy public street tree for the reason given, we need proof of the roots damaging the foundation. Proof shall include pictures of the roots growing through and damaging the foundation and written documentation from a structural engineer stating that the tree must be removed in order for the repairs to be completed. Until then the tree will not be removed. As the property owner you may also cut any of the trees roots back to your property line in order to prevent this damage in the future. Proof of the damage can be sent to Boston Parks & Recreation Department 3rd floor, 1010 Massachusetts Ave Boston, MA 02118 Attn: Urban Forestry Division or emailed to TREES@Boston.gov.

All public street trees in the City of Boston are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). For the BPRD Urban Forestry Division to remove a live and healthy public street tree for the reason given, we need proof of the roots damaging the foundation. Proof shall include pictures of the roots growing through and damaging the foundation and written documentation from a structural engineer stating that the tree must be removed in order for the repairs to be completed. Until then the tree will not be removed. As the property owner you may also cut any of the trees roots back to your property line in order to prevent this damage in the future. There are also large shrubs up against the house that could have roots damaging pipes and or the foundation. Proof of the damage can be sent to Boston Parks & Recreation Department 3rd floor, 1010 Massachusetts Ave Boston, MA 02118 Attn: Urban Forestry Division or emailed to TREES@Boston.gov.

All public street trees in the City of Boston are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). For the BPRD Urban Forestry Division to remove a live and healthy public street tree for the reason given, we need proof of the roots damaging the pipes. Proof shall include pictures of the roots growing through and damaging the pipes and written documentation from a licensed plumber stating that the tree must be removed in order for the repairs to be completed. Until then the tree will not be removed. Proof of the damage can be sent to Boston Parks & Recreation Department 3rd floor, 1010 Massachusetts Ave Boston, MA 02118 Attn: Urban Forestry Division or emailed to TREES@Boston.gov.

All public street trees in the City of Boston are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). For the BPRD Urban Forestry Division to remove a live and healthy tree for the reason given, we need proof of the roots damaging the foundation. Proof shall include pictures of the roots growing through and damaging the foundation and written documentation from a structural engineer stating that the tree must be removed in order for the repairs to be completed. Until then the tree will not be removed. Proof of the damage can be sent to Boston Parks & Recreation Department 3rd floor, 1010 Massachusetts Ave Boston, MA 02118 Attn: Urban Forestry Division or emailed to TREES@Boston.gov.

All public street trees in the City of Boston are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). There is no indication that the tree is actively uprooting. The tree is alive and healthy and cannot be removed. According to Google streetview the tree has been leaning like that since at least 2007.

As stated in numerous prior case closure notes regarding this public shade tree. The tree is alive and healthy and cannot be removed for the reasons you are requesting it be removed for. All public street trees in the City of Boston and the state of Massachusetts are protected by state law (MGL CH 87). In the City of Boston, public trees are also protected by the Public Tree Ordinance which passed by the Boston City Council. Both MGL CH87 and the Public Tree Ordinance prevent the removal of a live public shade tree. There are currently 2 open sidewalk repair cases for this location. Case #101006237211 & #101006083193. The repair of sidewalks is handled by the Public Works Department, not the Urban Forestry Division. If you would like an update regarding the status of the repair of the sidewalk please contact Public Works Highway Division at 617-635-4900.

At the time of inspection, there appears to be no shifting of the rootplate or lifting of the sidewalk surrounding the tree. No work is needed at this time. According to Massachusetts General Law chapter 87 all public street trees are protected by state law. Furthermore, included are pictures from 3 years ago and 7 years ago. You'll see that it looks the same as it did then. It's leaning due to phototropism, which is a growth response from the tree growing towards the light.

Back Street is a private way. The City of Boston Urban Forestry Division does not maintain trees on private way roads. It is the abutting property owners responsibility to maintain trees on private ways.

Bag replaced. Bag was holding water, sometimes the bags become clogged with dirt and debris so we have to poke smaller holes to ensure water can still trickle out. Tree is under warranty until July 2026. After that the water bags will be removed by the contractor.

Bag was holding water, sometimes the bags become clogged with dirt and debris so we have to poke smaller holes to ensure water can still trickle out. Tree is under warranty until July 2026. After that the water bags will be removed by the contractor. 1 of the 3 small trees is out of warranty and will not have its bag replaced.

BPRD does not install any form of raised planters in tree pits. Any ones that you see around the city have been installed by residents on their own accord.

BPRD is not responsible for christmas tree collection. Please refer to this link on christmas tree collection instructions. https://www.boston.gov/departments/public-works/leaf-and-yard-waste-schedule#christmas-tree-collection

BPRD is not responsible for picking up private christmas trees. From the City of Boston website, "We collect Christmas trees curbside on your normal trash day from December 29, 2025 - January 9, 2026. If you have two collection days per week, Christmas trees will be collected on your first collection day of the week."
